task: I’ve got a promo update that may need to go out even if the weather knocks out power or slows a few locations down. Can you help me think through the safest way to keep the update moving and what to prioritize if parts of the setup become unavailable?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — safest approach is to treat it like a **controlled fallback rollout**.

### Priority order if things get shaky
1. **Lock the source of truth**
   - Final promo details
   - Dates/times
   - Eligible locations
   - Any exclusions or weather-related changes

2. **Protect customer-facing accuracy first**
   - Landing page / offer page
   - Promo code
   - CTA links
   - Any store-hours or availability messaging

3. **Update downstream comms**
   - Email
   - SMS
   - Social
   - Partner-facing notes

4. **Notify internal teams**
   - Support
   - Ops
   - Store/field contacts
   - Anyone who may get customer questions

5. **QA the critical path**
   - Code works
   - Links resolve
   - Dates match everywhere
   - No stale hours or location info

### If parts become unavailable
Use a simple split:
- **Must ship now:** anything that affects what customers see or whether the promo is valid
- **Can wait:** nice-to-have copy, secondary channels, noncritical formatting, internal polish

### Safest operating rule
If weather could change things again, avoid making scattered edits in multiple places first.  
**Update the master/source doc, then the page, then email/other channels.** That keeps you from creating mismatches.

### Biggest risk to watch
The first thing that usually breaks is:
- old promo code still live somewhere
- mismatched timing
- location/hours info that’s no longer true

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page “normal day vs disruption day” checklist** you can use while you’re mid-rollout.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is the right idea. But I’d want it a little more operational, like what to do when the power/internet issue is actually happening, not just the general order.

What I’d prioritize first:
- source of truth locked
- customer-facing page/code/hours corrected
- anything that affects live eligibility
- then email/SMS/social after that
- internal support note last, but still before launch if possible

The part I’d add is a fallback chain:
- if full rollout is blocked, ship only the page update and hold the rest
- if the page is blocked, at least update partner/store comms so nobody is working off stale info
- if a location is down, mark it out of scope instead of forcing a half-correct send

Also, I’d want a quick checklist for weather disruption specifically:
- confirm which locations are open
- confirm any shortened hours
- confirm who can approve edits if the main person is offline
- save a copy of the final version somewhere accessible off-site
- have a backup way to push the update from mobile if needed

I’ve had the same kind of scramble during a hurricane warning — the part that saves you is not working harder, it’s having a clean fallback and not letting multiple versions of the promo drift apart. 

If you want, make it into a tighter step-by-step runbook with an actual decision tree: power available / no power / partial location closure.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter **weather-disruption runbook** with a simple decision tree.

## 1) First 10 minutes: stabilize
**Do these first, in order:**
1. **Lock the source of truth**
   - Final promo copy
   - Eligible locations
   - Hours
   - Code / offer rules
   - Approval owner

2. **Check what’s actually down**
   - Power?
   - Internet?
   - CMS access?
   - Email/SMS platform access?
   - Any locations closed or operating reduced hours?

3. **Freeze nonessential edits**
   - No parallel changes in multiple channels
   - One owner, one version

4. **Decide the launch path**
   - Full rollout
   - Partial rollout
   - Hold

---

## 2) Priority order if you can still move
If something has to ship, prioritize in this order:

1. **Customer-facing page / code / hours**
2. **Eligibility rules**  
   - especially if some locations are closed or limited
3. **Email / SMS / social**
4. **Internal support note**
5. **Polish / secondary updates**

---

## 3) Decision tree

### A. **Power + internet available**
**Best case.**
- Update source doc
- Push page first
- Confirm code/hours/eligibility
- Send email/SMS/social
- Send internal support note
- QA all links and dates

### B. **Power available, but internet is spotty or tools are partially down**
**Partial rollout mode.**
- If the **page can be updated**, do that first
- If page is blocked, **do not force downstream sends yet**
- Update **partner/store/support comms** so people aren’t using stale info
- Hold email/SMS/social until the page or source can be verified

### C. **No power / no reliable access**
**Fallback mode.**
- Use the last confirmed source-of-truth copy
- Mark affected locations **out of scope** if needed
- Send only the minimum comms that can prevent confusion:
  - internal support note
  - partner/store note
- Hold customer-facing sends unless you can verify the live page/code/hours

---

## 4) Fallback rules
Use these rules to keep the rollout safe:

- **If the page is blocked:**  
  update partner/store/internal comms first, and hold customer sends

- **If a location is down:**  
  exclude it explicitly instead of letting it look like it should participate

- **If the main approver is offline:**  
  use the backup approver already named in the runbook

- **If the promo details are changing fast:**  
  ship only the minimum safe update, then wait on the rest

---

## 5) Weather-specific checklist
Before launch, confirm:

- Which locations are open
- Which locations have shortened hours
- Whether any locations are excluded
- Who can approve if the main person is offline
- Where the final version is saved off-site
- Whether you can push from mobile if needed

---

## 6) Simple “ship / hold” rule
**Ship now:**
- page
- code
- hours
- eligibility
- anything customers will see immediately

**Hold if needed:**
- email
- SMS
- social
- formatting tweaks
- noncritical internal cleanup

---

## 7) Practical operating rule
If conditions are unstable, don’t try to “finish everything.”  
Try to **make one version true everywhere that matters**.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checklist** with boxes you can literally use during a storm scramble.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is closer. But I’d tighten it one more notch into something I can actually use in the middle of a scramble.

What I’d want added:
- a clearer **if/then** for who owns the call when the main approver is offline
- a hard rule for **what counts as safe enough to ship** vs. what gets held
- a tiny section for **mobile-only fallback**, since that’s the real ugly case

Also, I’d swap the emphasis a bit:
- **source of truth + approver** first
- **page/code/hours** second
- **location status / exclusions** third
- **email/SMS/social** only after the live stuff is verified
- **support note** before launch if there’s any chance customers will call in

The weather piece is good, but I’d make it even more operational, like:
- if power is out at the office, use the last approved version only
- if internet is unstable, don’t touch multiple channels at once
- if a location status changes, remove it from scope immediately instead of trying to patch it later

I had the same kind of mess during a hurricane warning — once the updates start drifting across channels, you spend more time cleaning up than shipping. The real fix is a clean fallback path and one person owning the final version.

So yeah, turn this into a short runbook with:
1. decision tree
2. approval fallback
3. ship/hold rule
4. mobile backup steps

That’d be the useful version.

turns.agent_response:
## Weather disruption promo runbook

### 1) Decision tree
**Power + internet available**
- Use current approved source of truth
- Confirm approver
- Update page/code/hours
- Confirm location status/exclusions
- Verify live flow
- Then send email/SMS/social
- Send support note before launch if customers may call in

**Power available, internet unstable**
- Freeze multi-channel edits
- Update only the page if you can verify it
- If page cannot be verified, hold customer sends
- Update internal/support/partner notes only if they reduce confusion

**No power / no reliable access**
- Use last approved version only
- Do not edit multiple channels
- Remove any changed location from scope
- Hold email/SMS/social
- Send only the minimum internal note if it can be done safely

---

### 2) Approval fallback
**Primary approver offline**
- Use named backup approver
- If no backup is reachable, hold launch
- Do not split approval across multiple people in real time

**Rule**
- One owner
- One approved version
- One final call

---

### 3) Ship / hold rule
**Safe enough to ship**
- Source of truth is locked
- Approver is confirmed
- Page/code/hours are correct
- Live eligibility is correct
- Location exclusions are explicit
- Link path is verified

**Hold**
- Any channel that would create mismatch
- Any update that depends on unstable access
- Any location that cannot be confirmed
- Any partial send that would confuse customers

---

### 4) Mobile-only fallback
- Open the last approved version only
- Confirm you can access the live page/admin tool before changing anything
- Make one update at a time
- Recheck page/code/hours after each change
- If you cannot verify, stop and hold the rest
- Save screenshots or a copy of the final approved version if possible

---

### 5) Priority order
1. Source of truth + approver
2. Page / code / hours
3. Location status / exclusions
4. Email / SMS / social
5. Support note
6. Cleanup / polish

---

### 6) Hard stop rules
- If a location is down, remove it from scope immediately
- If internet is unstable, do not touch multiple channels at once
- If the page cannot be verified, do not send customer-facing comms
- If approval is unclear, do not launch

If you want, I can compress this into a **true one-page call sheet** with just checkboxes and if/then lines.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is basically there. I’d still want the one-page call sheet version, though — cleaner, less prose, more like something I can glance at while everything’s half on fire.

Main thing I’d tighten:
- make the approval fallback even more explicit: who is backup, and if backup is offline, no launch. no gray area.
- put the hard stop rules right at the top, not buried near the bottom.
- for mobile-only, add a line for “don’t start a second edit until the first one is verified live.” That’s the part people screw up.

If you can turn this into a checkbox sheet with maybe 10-15 lines max, that’d be the usable version.

turns.agent_response:
## Weather disruption promo call sheet

### HARD STOPS
- [ ] If approval is unclear: **do not launch**
- [ ] If primary approver is offline: use **named backup approver**
- [ ] If backup approver is offline: **no launch**
- [ ] If page cannot be verified live: **hold customer-facing sends**
- [ ] If internet is unstable: **do not edit multiple channels**
- [ ] If a location is down: **remove it from scope immediately**

### ORDER
- [ ] 1. Source of truth locked
- [ ] 2. Approver confirmed
- [ ] 3. Page / code / hours updated
- [ ] 4. Location status / exclusions confirmed
- [ ] 5. Verify live flow
- [ ] 6. Send email / SMS / social
- [ ] 7. Send support note before launch if customers may call in

### SHIP / HOLD
- [ ] **Ship only if:** source is locked, approver confirmed, live page verified, eligibility correct, exclusions explicit
- [ ] **Hold if:** any channel is stale, any location is unconfirmed, or any step depends on unstable access

### MOBILE-ONLY FALLBACK
- [ ] Open **last approved version only**
- [ ] Make **one edit at a time**
- [ ] **Do not start a second edit until the first is verified live**
- [ ] Save a screenshot / copy of the final approved version if possible
- [ ] If you cannot verify live: **stop and hold**

### FINAL CHECK
- [ ] One owner
- [ ] One approved version
- [ ] One final call

user_simulator.text:
Yeah — this works. Clean enough to actually use.

The hard stops up top is the right move, and the mobile line is the part people usually blow past, so that’s good.

If I were using this mid-scramble, I’d probably want one tiny thing later: a slot for the backup approver’s name so it’s not just implied. But as a call sheet, this is solid.
